author: @ACTSRevolution
text: A suggestion for Rob if he hasn't already gotten into the remastering:  Videos are inherently illegitimate, being analog.  Why not use each video's strengths as judged by your viewers who have pored over them in realtime?  Just post the comment sections on google docs so viewers can text-search through them for questions they have.  If a question is not already broached in the comments, then that issue was never of interest to  the material covered - no relevance.  RMS videos have had some downright sharp adherents and their comments not only have illustrated what the video was trying to accomplish, but often have even better information and ideas than the video was able to cover -- the whole reason YouTube videos go so well with comment sections!

author: @55Ramius
text: My first thought was maybe leaving the videos but make the videos searchable by titling the video specifically to the subject matter.  In case people do not know, you can click on anybody's channel name and of course, you see play list, etc. You also see a search bar. Now if your video is about wind generation and you title it something like " How to make your own electricity" , then your search for WIND generation will be lost in the other forms of videos relating to generating instead of being specific. Unless you have thousands of videos on wind generation, a person should be able to zero in quickly on the one they want to see. Has worked well for me when searching people's channels. Good luck which ever way you go.   : )

author: @robertmiller5135
text:
Please don't take down any videos, but rather simply redo some of the videos that you feel need refreshing, and reissue them. The comments to the various videos are also a source of information and if you delete them, those would be lost. May I suggest an overarching website that allows a reorganisation according to topic, where you can organise the videos outside of youtube system, using youtube it as a video database. This would allow you to keep the old videos, their comments, and then add a new/or better video while creating a very organised framework for the user. People could still browse the video files on youtube but the website would give you a chance to organise everything, even allowing you to add comments/diagrams/or additional information relevant to the videos later on. 

By the way, I have watched some of your older videos and I haven't found them "unwatchable" at all.

author: @alaingrignon
text: @Rob, a word of caution. Deleting any video content will affect your monetization metrics for all the rest of your videos. Your viewing history for each of those videos contributes to your overall ranking in searches and advertising revenue. You are almost better to redo new videos that combine a bunch of old stuff, post them into playlists, keep the old shorter videos and place those into an "old stuff" playlist. That way you double up your content and rankings. I fully support reorganizing though as I myself (have been watching you for over 5 years and can't find alot of the older videos that could help me today).

author: @aadhithvarathan5432
text:
Sir, I have been doing research on electric motors and EVs for quite a while now. I really appreciate your idea of creating an external commutator ring and replicating the 3 phase shape. I am also working on a cheaper ESC for BLDC motors. However, some proponents of electronics will say "I cannot accept this as this is still a brushed motor". 

Would using DPDT magnetic reed switches work? We can place 3 DPDT reed switches side by side and place mini magnets on the commutator rings just like the 3 phase square wave you have placed with the brass and resin. This way we can reduce frictional losses of this mechanical speed controller for BLDC motors

This would be a completely brushless alternative and is much cheaper and more versatile than the electronic speed controller.

author: @scotttovey
text:
Having watched many videos on building a YouTube channel and growing a YouTube Channel, the number 1 tip they give is; don't delete old videos.

There is no problem with uploading new versions of older one's that may need better quality; but don't delete the old ones. Just add update, revisited or improved quality to the title of the video you are redoing.

The thing to remember is that the search ai already knows where your old videos are; it doesn't know where your new videos are and having the old video point to the new video will give you a better click through rate and the click through rate is what determines your ad revenue rate.

author: @woofwoof6721
text:
G'day Rob,
There seems to be a lot of calls for not deleting anything and I think I agree.
What I would really like to see is a wiki set up that has detailed descriptions of all the videos as individual pages with keywords. This would then be searchable and pages with playlists linking the videos could also be added. It could also be set up with counters that could show which videos need the most work so that you can more efficiently direct your energies. You could probably even add a url to the description of the videos to allow people to request an update.
You could also open it up to being edited by subscribers or the public in general and crowd source some of the labour. Should only be a matter of setting up a description template that can be filled in and edited, (would probably help if all the videos on the youtube channel had their numbers in the title.... :).
You have a wealth of info on your channel and I think a large interested community that would be willing to help organise it. It just needs an accessible database like a wiki to allow interested parties to be able to contribute.
If you aren't sure how to set one up, once again I'm pretty sure that there will be a few people around that would be willing to give you a hand getting it going. I've been doing my own little database on my computer so that I can search things.
Hope this helps.

author: @toadyuk8391
text:
My suggestion is this. 
1. Don’t remove anything, because you never know what small hint in a video helps someone. Plus the idea of remaking is sound but often it’s easy to get started and harder to finish.

2. You don’t need to remove content. You could organise the videos into playlists, which would help people find content easily. You also wouldn’t lose your stats.

3. Once you have a series of related playlists, electroplating, battery ingredients, battery making etc , you can then have a look and remake anything that really does need a sharper focus. You could then if you felt the need take the others away.

4. By not removing anything you preserve an archive, however it’s a bit like tidying up your tools. Look I have some 3000 drill bits, sets, odds and sods, long special drills, tin coated, fresner bits etc. Some of these are even from my grandad and my dad. We never threw a drill away ! The useless ones we still store in a box, why you ask ? Well I find they make useful stock for engraving bits, punches, ground tips for one off jobs and so forth. If you apply the same approach, I can find my special drills, I can find my main drills, I can find my backup set and the stock box.

replies.text:
@Teknopottu I'll give them the benefit of the doubt on this, regarding previous output. Give them some time and space. Patience.
I doubt there'll be too much 'remaking' as  early stuff was so good, any few glitches were easily resolved by viewer efforts, and a plus of that was it encouraged viewers to have a go at things themselves to 'see how it works' and learn something. That can't be a bad thing. Being spoon-fed is only useful for a while before you want to take a bite of something with a little more substance.  ;-)

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text: @bikerfirefarter7280 There is truth in your saying. But I've just grown to wait for that spoonful of goodness almost every day. And it makes my day most of the times. Would hate to have a decrease in new stuff.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
@Teknopottu While you're waiting, try doing some of Rob's suggested experimenting. Or do some home-grown ones. Solar heat stores have lots of uses, I made a linear concentrator on my allotment from an old curved shower door and a copper pipe inside a glass tube (old fluorescent light). The water gets scalding hot. I'm going to try some graphene additive in it, then add a sterling cycle engine.
Meanwhile, go and play with yourself.  ;-)

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text: @bikerfirefarter7280 Solar air  heater is on project table, gathering tin cans now. So is chargable powerbank with hand crank, first I made couple of years ago. When my vacation hits, there is some time for projects but daily job consumes too much time.

author: @scantrain5007
text:
Dear Rob,
I don't think it's necessary to re-load the videos. In my mind it's enough if you offer somehow / somewhere (your Web-Site?) an excel-file with the title of video, short discription and the link. 
Even to create those Excel File is a lot of work! But I think that viewers who are really intersted are willing to spend a small money for this.
By the way: In this File you can put the links to your excellent books as well: Very important stuff for building batteries and supercaps.

author: @karlmyers6518
text: I had a feeling you were upto something.  I was shocked to not to see a video last night but not disappointed.

author: @maquabear5242
text: Yes! I agree with you about stuff being hard to find. I was trying to share one of your videos about rebuilding a battery, and after ten minutes of scrolling on my phone, I gave up.  I love your channel. KEEP UP THE GOOD WORK!

author: @etherlonX
text:
I certainly appreciate your earlier vids as they are what got me into home battery construction and chemistry in general :)  It has been a great addition to my electronics and maker hobbies. In fact, about a third of my workshop is now a chem lab (for making batteries)! I for one would love to see a revamped battery list with some new takes on the various types batteries, and how to make the chemicals for them if they are not available in an immediate form. For example, making nickel hydroxide for nickel/iron batteries, zinc bromine from sodium bromine and zinc sulfate (the zinc bromine vids are actually good quality already). Another area that might be good to look at is electrode construction for the various batteries. It seems easy enough to make a battery but if you want something that performs half decent you need good electrodes. As an idea for zinc bromine batteries maybe a way to make glassy carbon or vitreous carbon electrodes in the kiln?

Anyway, love the channel, learnt a lot, and looking forward to watching many more vids to come.

Cheers!

author: @martinwinfield2935
text: Thanks for your thoughts, I think that you may have made yourself a whole load of very time consuming work. I know this from attempting to categorizing my own non published videos.  Perhaps time spent on sorting out into a very precise index for each topic. Then all would be easy to locate. Nothing at all wrong with leaving early content, we all learn and improve with our experiences.

author: @MrShakaib98
text: Hi Bob, it was the batteries which got me interested in your channel, please don't remove any important videos from your channel. I wanted to see your Twizzy but sadly that project did not get going due to some reasons. Good luck for reorganising your videos and thank you very much for your efforts. Gudday as they say down under.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Isn't it a shame that with the internet we've forgotten about library science?  Categorizing and organizing information for sensible retrieval is difficult.  For many people it was once a full time job.  Good job for recognizing the issue and tackling the problem.

If you add a # before all the show numbers it will help a LOT

text: I love this idea!

author: @angelusmendez5084
text: Awesome!

author: @nw7696
text: Very nice, great idea for all to follow. Back then, those of you who were posting content probably never realized how immense YouTube would have become. Thanks for all your hard work and for sharing all of your great ideas with us. Just remember to take some "personal time" out so that you don't burn out. 😊👍🏻

author: @AtlasReburdened
text: I've happily watched the videos from back when you filmed on a potato with loose microphone wires, but I'm the type of guy that could drink bile if I knew it was good for me, so I don't know that the quality of the old videos not bothering me is any metric to weight in the decision to remake them. I hope it boosts viewership.

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Excellent ideas Rob, I love watching some of your old videos and you can see just how much you have changed as well as the video formats, quality and quantity. I'm going to throw an idea out there, it may not be feasible but you have Luke and his computer skills. Have you thought about using those skills to set up a forum where not only can members and subscribers chat and help out each other's ideas but also your video links can be categorised into their content types for people to go back and watch all videos related to batteries or capacitors or inks or energy harvesting etc..
Just thought I would throw that out their if you and Luke have the time, even if it's done a bit at a time as you go through them all.
By the way, the camera I won turned up just over an hour ago, thank you my dear friend from the very bottom of my heart 💖

replies.author: @BigRich2580
replies.text:
That is along the same lines as I was thinking Steve, but far better put.
Was thinking of an online storage hub. Dropbox esk type thing, that members, subs and viewers can not only dip into for knowledgeable help but also the option of discussing their own project hurdles for resourceful people like Robert, Luke and all of us who have that brainfart moment to help with, whilst also being able to share their learnt from mistakes, errors, workarounds and such..........see you put it so much better

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @BigRich2580 appreciate the thumbs up to the idea. I think that Rob has so much to share and being able to discuss these ideas amongst the huge community here could spark something great. Would be an amazing legacy for generations to come.

replies.author: @BigRich2580
replies.text: @stevetobias4890 could not agree more with you

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @BigRich2580 let's hope that Rob and Luke both feel the same 🙄

author: @ffaubert1
text: Thank you for thinking about your subscribers time, but to be honest,  most of us would tune in to watch you boil pasta. Somehow you would make it interesting, teach us something  and provide us food for thought (OK, bad pun, but you know what I'm saying).

author: @ezequieladrianminniti6252
text: I think you shouldn't take down any video, because that will create a drop in the visualization metrics and will probably affect the advertising. Best recommendation is to better organize into playlists/categories and of course redo the old ones but do not delete them.

replies.author: @czechyorker
replies.text: Makes sense. Those videos have a bad audio quality, but I doubt you will try to fix that. Instead you may want to redo the video and post it with the same name and adding (edited) or (new version) in the title. Just a thought.

replies.author: @excitedbox5705
replies.text:
That is a very good point. The channel overall views will drop and the algorithm will hit him hard for that. It also changes how much he gets paid per view in the future (at least it used to). I think even reposting the same video counts against you. I think reorganizing them into playlists and maybe even embedding them into his website in categories is a better idea.
I am a web designer and if he needs help building up a video library on his website I can help him do so.

replies.author: @asstronix6657
replies.text: Yes, absolutely indeed 👍🏼

author: @PACstove
